questions.question_text:
A supermarket wants to compare how many customers visited on each day of the week.
Which type of graph is most suitable?

options.why: A sector graph shows each day as a share of the whole, not an easy day-to-day comparison of totals.
Days of the week are separate categories, so joining them with a line to show a trend is not the purpose.
Stem-and-leaf plots show the shape of a single numerical dataset, not a comparison of categories.

questions.solution_text:
The purpose is to compare the totals for separate categories (days).
A column graph is most suitable.

questions.question_text:
A nurse records a patient’s temperature every hour and wants to show how it changes through the day.
Which type of graph is most suitable?

options.why: A column graph compares separate categories rather than showing continuous change over time.
A sector graph shows parts of a whole, not how a value changes across time.
A dot plot shows the shape of a dataset but does not track a value over time.

questions.solution_text:
Temperature over time is continuous data changing over time.
A line graph is most suitable.

questions.question_text:
A council wants one display showing what percentage of its waste is recycled, composted or sent to landfill, out of the total.
Which type of graph is most suitable?

options.why: A line graph shows change over time, not each category as a share of a total.
A dot plot shows the spread of numerical data, not percentages of a whole.
A stem-and-leaf plot displays numerical values, not parts of a whole.

questions.solution_text:
The purpose is to show parts of a whole as percentages.
A sector graph is most suitable.

questions.question_text:
A coach records the number of goals each player scored in a season and wants to see how the goal counts are spread, including any outliers.
Which type of graph is most suitable?

options.why: A sector graph shows shares of a whole and hides the spread and any outliers.
A line graph shows change over time; the goal counts are not a time series.
A pictogram compares category totals but does not clearly reveal the spread and outliers.

questions.solution_text:
The data is numerical and the purpose is to show its spread and outliers.
A dot plot is most suitable.

options.why: It shows shares of a whole; here the purpose is to compare the counts for each plant type.
A histogram is for numerical scores with touching columns, not separate plant-type categories.

options.text: A line graph with the points not joined
options.why: The problem is the graph type for categorical data, not just whether the points are joined.

questions.solution_text:
Plant type is categorical data, so joining the points with a line is meaningless.
A column graph should be used, because it compares the count for each plant type.
